text: laparoscopic robotic assisted transgastric resection of gastric gist. This is a seventy-one year old male with hyperlipidemia and obesity that consulted for nausea, vomiting and left upper quadrant pain. During the workup, a US and a CT scan showed a four centimeter submucosal lesion protruding into the gastric lumen at the gastroesophageal junction. The endoscopic ultrasound confirmed the diagnosis.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic robotic system to resect a submucosal tumor at the gastroesophageal junction. Trochors are placed, and a Nathanson liver retractor is positioned in the epigastrium. Short gastric vessels are mobilized from the splenic hilum to the gastroesophageal junction using a bipolar device and hook. The left pillar of the crass is dissected, and the fat pad is resected to expose the fundus. The lesion is identified as mostly intraluminal in the posterior stomach. An intraoperative endoscopy confirms the lesion's location, and a 52 French bougie is inserted. An anterior gastrotomy is performed four centimeters from the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon lifts the tumor from the posterior aspect of the gastric wall. Two Endo GIA staplers with purple loads are fired across the tumor, ensuring a good margin of mucosa. The lesion is extracted using an endopouch and sent for histopathological analysis. A frozen section confirms a negative margin with no infiltration at the suture line. The suture line is then reinforced with 3-0 PDS sutures.

The surgeon closes the anterior gastrotomy using running 3-0 VLOG sutures and interrupted 3-0 PDS sutures. An intraoperative endoscopy confirms no intragastric bleeding or leaks, with good passage through the gastroesophageal junction. The surgical field shows no blood loss, and no drains are visible.
